name: Name That Country
description: Identify countries from their silhouette and position on the globe: every UN member and observer state, plus Kosovo and Taiwan.

fields.extra: Surrounds two enclave microstates, San Marino and Vatican City.
About 4300 km long but on average only 180 km wide.
Four main islands: Hokkaido, Honshu, Shikoku, Kyushu.
Covers nearly half of South America and borders every country on the continent except Chile and Ecuador.
Shares the Scandinavian Peninsula with Sweden; the jagged western edge is its fjord coast.
Fragmented southern tip of the Balkans with thousands of islands in the Aegean.
The crescent shape wraps around Bosnia and Herzegovina.
On the Adriatic between Montenegro and Greece.
Pyrenees microstate wedged between France and Spain.
The long western tail reaches through the Alps toward Liechtenstein and Switzerland.
Landlocked between Poland, Ukraine, Russia, and the Baltics.
Between France and the Netherlands on the North Sea.
The wedge inside the crescent of Croatia; nearly landlocked except a 20 km coastline at Neum.
On the western Black Sea coast, south of Romania across the Danube.
Landlocked, tucked between Germany, Poland, Austria, and Slovakia.
The Jutland Peninsula plus islands, pointing up from Germany toward Norway and Sweden.
Northernmost of the three Baltic states, just south of Finland.
Between Sweden and Russia; sometimes described as shaped like a one-armed maiden.
Often called the Hexagon.
Landlocked in the Carpathian Basin; Lake Balaton is the notch in the west.
North Atlantic island just below the Arctic Circle.
The highlighted area excludes Northern Ireland, which is part of the United Kingdom.
Landlocked between Serbia, North Macedonia, Albania, and Montenegro; declared independence in 2008.
Middle of the three Baltic states; the Gulf of Riga bites into its coast.
Alpine microstate between Switzerland and Austria; doubly landlocked.
Southernmost of the three Baltic states, bordering Poland and the Kaliningrad exclave.
Small wedge between Belgium, Germany, and France.
Small island group south of Sicily.
Landlocked sliver between Romania and Ukraine.
City-state on the French Riviera; second smallest country in the world.
Small Adriatic state between Croatia, Bosnia, Serbia, Kosovo, and Albania.
North Sea coast east of Britain; much of it below sea level.
Landlocked Balkan state north of Greece; renamed from Macedonia in 2019.
Broad rectangle on the Baltic between Germany and the former USSR states.
The narrow Atlantic strip of Iberia, west of Spain.
The Carpathians curl through it; the Danube delta is on its Black Sea coast.
Largest country on Earth, spanning eleven time zones.
Microstate entirely surrounded by Italy, near Rimini.
Landlocked at the center of the Balkans; Belgrade sits at the Sava-Danube confluence.
The eastern half of former Czechoslovakia, north of Hungary.
Chicken-shaped state between Italy, Austria, Hungary, and Croatia.
Most of the Iberian Peninsula, with Portugal cut out of the west side.
The eastern, Baltic-facing side of the Scandinavian Peninsula.
Landlocked Alpine state; the notch in the south is Italy around Lake Lugano.
Largest country entirely within Europe; Crimea hangs into the Black Sea.
Great Britain plus Northern Ireland.
Smallest country in the world, an enclave inside Rome.
The thin Wakhan Corridor in the northeast reaches out to touch China.
Small landlocked Caucasus state west of Azerbaijan.
On the Caspian Sea; includes the Nakhchivan exclave separated by Armenia.
Island state in the Persian Gulf, connected to Saudi Arabia by a causeway.
The Ganges delta, almost entirely surrounded by India.
Himalayan kingdom sandwiched between China and India, east of Nepal.
Two small pieces on the north coast of Borneo, surrounded by Malaysia.
Between Thailand and Vietnam; the lake in the middle is the Tonle Sap.
The lighter green areas (including Taiwan) are claimed but not controlled by Beijing.
Eastern Mediterranean island south of Turkey; the pan-handle points toward Syria.
Caucasus state on the Black Sea, between Russia and Turkey.
World's largest archipelago state, straddling the Equator across more than 17000 islands.
Between the Caspian Sea and the Persian Gulf.
Mesopotamia; the Tigris and Euphrates meet near its short Gulf coastline.
Narrow Mediterranean strip tapering south to the Red Sea port of Eilat.
Nearly landlocked; only a short Red Sea coast at Aqaba.
Largest landlocked country in the world.
Small wedge at the head of the Persian Gulf, between Iraq and Saudi Arabia.
Mountainous Central Asian state east of Uzbekistan; mostly Tian Shan range.
The only landlocked country in Southeast Asia, stretched along the Mekong.
Small Mediterranean strip north of Israel, west of Syria.
Split in two: the peninsula below Thailand and the northern strip of Borneo.
Chain of coral atolls southwest of India; lowest-lying country on Earth.
Landlocked between only two neighbors, Russia and China.
Kite shape with a long tail running down the Andaman coast; formerly Burma.
Himalayan strip between India and China; home to Mount Everest.
The northern half of the Korean Peninsula, bordering China and briefly Russia.
Southeast corner of Arabia; a detached tip on the Strait of Hormuz is the Musandam exclave.
The Indus valley, between Iran, Afghanistan, and India.
Two territories: the West Bank and the Gaza Strip.
Archipelago of over 7000 islands; Luzon in the north, Mindanao in the south.
Thumb-shaped peninsula jutting north from Saudi Arabia into the Persian Gulf.
Most of the Arabian Peninsula; the Empty Quarter desert fills its southeast.
City-state on an island at the tip of the Malay Peninsula.
The southern half of the Korean Peninsula; Jeju Island lies off its south coast.
Teardrop island off the southeast tip of India.
Between Turkey and Iraq, with a short Mediterranean coast.
Sweet-potato-shaped island off the southeast coast of mainland China.
Mountainous Central Asian state; the Pamirs fill its eastern half.
Often compared to an elephant's head, with the trunk running down the Malay Peninsula.
Eastern half of Timor island, north of Australia; also called East Timor.
The Anatolian Peninsula plus a small piece of Europe across the Bosporus.
Mostly Karakum Desert, on the Caspian Sea north of Iran.
Seven emirates on the south shore of the Persian Gulf, between Saudi Arabia and Oman.
One of only two doubly landlocked countries (with Liechtenstein).
S-shaped strip down the east side of Indochina.
Southwest corner of Arabia; the island far to its east is Socotra.
Largest country in Africa.
The detached block to the north is the Cabinda exclave.
Narrow north-south strip between Togo and Nigeria.
Landlocked; mostly Kalahari, north of South Africa.
Landlocked in the Sahel, north of Ghana; formerly Upper Volta.
Small state at the north end of Lake Tanganyika, just south of Rwanda.
Atlantic island group about 600 km off the coast of Senegal.
Elongated triangle at the hinge of West and Central Africa, on the Gulf of Guinea.
Landlocked at almost the exact center of Africa.
Landlocked; its shrinking namesake lake sits on the western border.
Small volcanic islands between Mozambique and Madagascar.
The smaller Congo, west of the river; capital Brazzaville faces Kinshasa across the water.

fields.back: The Democratic Republic of the Congo
fields.extra: The larger Congo, second biggest country in Africa; formerly Zaire.
On the Gulf of Guinea between Liberia and Ghana; also called Ivory Coast.
Small state on the Bab-el-Mandeb strait, wedged between Eritrea, Ethiopia, and Somalia.
The Sinai Peninsula on the right connects it to Asia.
A mainland block plus Bioko island, where the capital Malabo sits.
Long Red Sea coastline north of the Horn; independent from Ethiopia since 1993.
Small kingdom nearly surrounded by South Africa; formerly Swaziland.
Most populous landlocked country in the world, at the center of the Horn of Africa.
Straddles the Equator on the Atlantic, between Equatorial Guinea and Congo.
Sliver along the Gambia River, surrounded by Senegal; smallest mainland African country.
On the Gulf of Guinea; the long lake in the east is Volta, one of the largest reservoirs on Earth.
Croissant curving from the Atlantic inland, wrapping around Sierra Leone and Liberia.
Small Atlantic state between Senegal and Guinea, with the Bijagos islands offshore.
On the Equator; Lake Turkana pokes down from its northern border.
Entirely surrounded by South Africa; the country with the highest low point on Earth.
On the West African coast between Sierra Leone and Cote d'Ivoire.
The Gulf of Sidra takes a bite out of its long Mediterranean coast.
Fourth largest island in the world, off the southeast coast of Africa.
Thin strip running along the western shore of its namesake lake.
The Niger River bends through its middle; Timbuktu sits on that bend.
Mostly Sahara, on the Atlantic between Western Sahara and Senegal.
Indian Ocean island east of Madagascar; once home of the dodo.
Northwest corner of Africa, across the Strait of Gibraltar from Spain.
Long Indian Ocean coastline facing Madagascar across its namesake channel.
The thin strip reaching east is the Caprivi Strip, aimed at the Zambezi.
Landlocked Sahel state north of Nigeria, its similarly named neighbor.
Most populous country in Africa, on the inner corner of the Gulf of Guinea.
Small, hilly, and just south of the Equator; Burundi is its twin to the south.
Two small volcanic islands in the Gulf of Guinea; second smallest African state.
Westernmost point of mainland Africa; The Gambia slices into it along the river.
Scattered Indian Ocean islands northeast of Madagascar; smallest African country.
Between Guinea and Liberia; the capital Freetown has one of the world's largest natural harbors.
The Horn of Africa, wrapping around Ethiopia with the longest mainland coastline on the continent.
The hole in the middle is Lesotho.
The world's newest country, split from Sudan in 2011; the White Nile runs through it.
The northern of the two Sudans, on the Red Sea below Egypt; largest African country until 2011.
Touches all three African Great Lakes: Victoria, Tanganyika, and Malawi.
Narrow strip between Ghana and Benin, only about 55 km of coastline.
Northernmost country in Africa, pointing at Sicily.
Landlocked on the north shore of Lake Victoria.
Butterfly shape; Victoria Falls sits on its border with Zimbabwe.
Landlocked between the Zambezi and Limpopo rivers.
Twin islands at the north end of the Lesser Antilles.
Tapers from the Pampas down to Tierra del Fuego.
Chain of about 700 islands starting just off Florida.
Easternmost Caribbean island, sitting apart from the Antilles arc.
Small Caribbean-coast state below the Yucatan, east of Guatemala.
Landlocked since losing its coast to Chile in the 1880s; Lake Titicaca is on its border with Peru.
Second largest country in the world; the huge bay in the middle is Hudson Bay.
The only South American country with both Caribbean and Pacific coasts.
Between Nicaragua and Panama; famously abolished its army in 1948.
Long crocodile-shaped island, the largest in the Caribbean.
Mountainous Lesser Antilles island between Guadeloupe and Martinique; not the Dominican Republic.
Eastern two thirds of Hispaniola, shared with Haiti.
Named for the Equator; the Galapagos Islands lie 1000 km offshore.
Smallest Central American country and the only one without a Caribbean coast.
Spice Isle at the south end of the Lesser Antilles, just north of Trinidad.
Just south of Mexico; the notch in its northeast is Belize.
Westernmost of the three Guianas, between Venezuela and Suriname.
Western third of Hispaniola; the two peninsulas open like a crab claw toward Cuba.
Wide Caribbean coast between Guatemala and Nicaragua.
South of Cuba, third largest Caribbean island.
The Baja California peninsula parallels its Pacific coast.
Largest Central American country; the big lake near the Pacific side shares its name.
S-shaped isthmus connecting the Americas; runs east-west, not north-south.
One of South America's two landlocked countries, split by its namesake river.
Pacific coast of South America between Ecuador and Chile.
Smallest country in the Americas, two islands in the northern Lesser Antilles.
Lesser Antilles island between Martinique and Saint Vincent.
Main island plus a chain of Grenadines running toward Grenada.
Smallest independent country in South America; middle of the three Guianas.
Two islands just off the Venezuelan coast.
Small wedge between Brazil and Argentina on the Rio de la Plata.
On the Caribbean coast of South America; Lake Maracaibo opens to the sea in its northwest.
Two main islands, Viti Levu and Vanua Levu, plus hundreds more; the 180th meridian passes through it.
Atolls scattered across all four hemispheres, straddling both the Equator and the antimeridian.
Two parallel chains of atolls in Micronesia, including Bikini.
The Federated States: four island groups stretching across the Caroline Islands.
Smallest island country in the world, a single raised coral island near the Equator.
Two main islands separated by the Cook Strait.
Western edge of Micronesia, east of the Philippines.
Eastern half of New Guinea plus the Bismarck Archipelago and Bougainville.
The independent western islands; American Samoa lies just to the east.
Double chain of islands east of Papua New Guinea, including Guadalcanal.
The only remaining Pacific kingdom, south of Samoa.
Nine tiny atolls between Kiribati and Fiji; fourth smallest country.
Y-shaped chain of volcanic islands east of Australia, formerly the New Hebrides.
